revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[城郭]]における区画・区域|[[富山県]][[富山市]]の地名「総曲輪（そうがわ）」|総曲輪}} [[ファイル:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|江戸城総構え]] '''総構え'''（そうがまえ）は、[[城]]や[[砦]]の'''外郭'''（がいかく）、またはその囲まれた内部のこと。特に、城のほか[[城下町]]一帯も含めて外周を[[堀]]や[[石垣]]、[[土塁]]で囲い込んだ、日本の城郭構造をいう。'''惣構'''（そうがまえ）、'''総曲輪'''（そうぐるわ）、'''総郭'''（そうぐるわ）ともいう。 == 概要 == 日本では異民族の侵入が少なかったことや山地が国土の大半を占めることなどから、大陸に見られるような城壁都市は一部を除いて発展せず、野戦用の防御施設として作られた「[[城柵|柵]]」や武士の居館を堀や櫓で防備した「館（やかた、たて、たち）」が、中世には[[山城]]へと発展した。 近世にいたり、城郭が単なる軍事拠点のみならず政治的統治拠点としての役割を持つようになると、城下町や家臣団防備の目的で従来の城の機能的構成部分（内郭）から、さらにもう一重外側に防御線が設けられるようになった。これが総構えである。普通、「城」という場合は、内郭のみを指し、外郭である総構えは天然の地勢（山・河川）をも含むため、どこまでをいうのか不明瞭なものもあった。また総構えの堀は総堀（惣堀）と言うが、外堀と言われることも多い。ただし単に外堀と言った場合は、総構え堀を指す場合と本城の外側の堀を指す場合とがある。 [[後北条氏]]の拠点、[[小田原城]]の総構えは2里半（約9[[キロメートル|km]]）に及ぶ空堀と土塁で城下町全体を囲む長大なものであった{{refnest|group=注|小田原城の惣構について、[[西股総生]]は、 全体として集落の囲い込みよりも防御上の合理性に基づいて設定されたと評価している。[[板橋 (小田原市)|板橋]]の宿（中世東海道の宿）が惣構のラインからは除外される一方、城の東方は惣構を[[山王川]]のラインまでに設定しているが、山王川と城中心部との間にひろがる平野部に集落が密集しているとは考えにいことによる[[#西股2003|西股2003]]、27-29頁。}}。[[大坂城]]の外郭も周囲2里の長さで、[[大坂の陣#大坂冬の陣|大坂冬の陣]]では外郭南門の外側に出丸が造られ（[[真田丸]]）、徳川方は外郭内に1歩も侵入できなかったという。また江戸時代の江戸城外郭は最大で、堀・石垣・塀が渦状に配されて江戸市街の全てを囲んでいた。 総構えの典型は、中国の城や中世欧州の都市のように、都市全域を囲む堀と塁（城壁）にみることができる。中世都市の[[堺市|堺]]は三方を深さ3[[メートル|m]]、幅10m程の濠で囲み、木戸を設けて防御に備えていた。今でも遺構を見ることが出来る[[京都市|京都]]の「[[御土居]]」も典型的な総構えであり総延長は5里26町余（22.4km）にも及んだ{{refnest|group=注|「御土居」は、土塁とともに堀が構築されていた。土塁と堀の幅は各々約20mであった[[#中村2003|中村2003]]、316頁。}}。 ファイル:OdoiOmiya.jpg|[[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]大宮土居町の御土居堀 ファイル:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|姫路城総構えと街道沿いの町割り。文化3年（1806年）。 ファイル:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|[[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]]総構え
{{国土航空写真}} ファイル:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|福岡城の曲輪分界図 == 最古の遺構 == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部]] 現在判明している総構えの最古の遺構は、[[兵庫県]][[伊丹市]]にある[[伊丹城|有岡城]]（伊丹城）趾で、国指定の史跡1979年12月指定。『図説 中世城郭事典 三』p.36となっている『夢路を追って～官兵衛と村重～摂津⇔姫路』神戸新聞　2014.05.02 朝刊　20頁　西播。有岡城は、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]に伊丹氏により築城され伊丹城と称したが、[[天正]]年間に[[荒木村重]]により改修され総構えとなり有岡城と改称した。しかし10年ほどで廃城となっている。 尚、文明15年(1483年)に築城が始まった山科本願寺も巨大な土塁と水堀を用いて寺社町を囲う壮大な総構えを有しており{{refnest|group=注|山科本願寺は外郭線を三重にめぐらし、本願寺を核に求心的な構造をなしていた[[#福島2003|福島2003]]、327頁。}}、これを最古とする説もある。 File:Itamij4.jpg|文禄年間に伊丹の姿を描いた絵図 File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|現代の伊丹市の地図における総構えの範囲 {{-}} == 主な総構えの城 == {{Col| ;東北地方 * [[盛岡城]] * [[久保田城]] * [[米沢城]] * [[若松城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"1991 平成3 会津若松城総構に蒲生氏郷時代の大型障子堀 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[桧原城]][[#石田2009|石田2009]]、78頁。"[[伊達政宗]]は、桧原に桧原城築き、城下町を取り囲むように、直線的な外構を構築した。土塁と堀、[[虎口#枡形虎口|外桝形の虎口]]が築かれている。" ;関東地方 * [[水戸城]] * [[土浦城]] * [[館林城]] * [[倉賀野城]] * [[白井城]] | * [[宿阿内城]] * [[平井城]] * [[本佐倉城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"本佐倉城（[[千葉県]][[印旛郡]][[酒々井町]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[臼井城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"臼井城（千葉県[[佐倉市]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[岩槻城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、28頁。"岩槻城の惣構は、現在では市街地の中にごく一部を残すのみだが、戦前までは「岩槻の金屏風」と俗称される雄大な土塁が良好に遺存していた。" * [[滝の城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、19-20頁。"[[埼玉県]][[所沢市]]の滝ノ城も、長大な外郭線を有している。（中略）想定される敵主攻正面は北東方であり、そうした軍事的状況に対応するように外郭線が設定されている。この外郭線には数カ所に横矢の折れがあり、東面には横矢に援護された[[虎口#馬出|馬出]]も備えたらしい。（中略）技巧を駆使しているのは、広大な台地続きに対して防御線が長くならざるをえないことへの対処、と理解できる。" * [[江戸城]][[#岡田2008|岡田2008]]、35頁。"[[御茶ノ水]]の巨大な総構" * [[小田原城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、267頁。"1986 昭和61 小田原城北縁総構竜土院裏の発掘で障子堀確認 惣構障子堀の初見" ;中部地方 * [[甲府城]] * [[小岩嶽城]]{{refnest|group=注|小岩嶽城（現・[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]にある[[山城]]）には山麓部を囲繞する空堀（幅約15m・深さ約7m）がある[[#三島2000|三島2000]]、69-71頁。}} * [[長岡城]] * [[田中城]] | * [[西尾城]] * [[清洲城]] * [[犬山城]] * [[岐阜城]][[#髙田2003|髙田2003]]、66頁。"①外郭の堀・土塁は長良川沿岸部を除いてほぼ直線的なラインである。②外郭南側の端部は「権現山」裾部で終わっている。" * [[松ノ木城]][[#福島2000|福島2000]]、321頁。"松ノ木城の「外構」「惣構」" * [[金沢城]]＜天正一二年＞三月一三日付、羽柴秀吉書状『加越能古文叢』四一。"金沢之惣構" ;近畿地方 * [[桑名城]] * [[長浜城 (近江国)|長浜城]] * [[彦根城]] * [[伏見城]] * [[亀山城 (丹波国)|亀山城]] * [[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]] | * [[大坂城]][[#中井2008|中井2008]]、29頁。"豊臣秀吉の大坂城の構造（中略）本丸の外周に二の丸、三の丸が配され、さらにその外周は広大な総構となっていた。" * [[岸和田城]] * [[有岡城]] （伊丹城） * [[明石城]] * [[姫路城]][[#宮田2008|宮田2008]]、50-53頁。"西国将軍となった池田輝政の築いた姫路城は、長期の籠城も可能として町屋や寺町全域を外堀で囲い込んだ総構の城である。" ;中国地方 * [[岡山城]] * [[福山城 (備後国)|福山城]] * [[広島城]] * [[三次城]] * [[松江城]][[#徳永2016|徳永2016]]、259頁。"松江城下町遺跡の[[障子堀]]は、外堀のさらに外側に位置する" * [[萩城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、152頁。"萩城（中略）惣構：三ノ丸⑤を巨大な土塁・で固めた惣構とし、大身家臣団屋敷を包摂。外辺部曲輪⑥に町屋地区・中下級家臣団屋敷を包摂し、三ノ丸⑤と共に整備った（ママ）街区で集約化" | ;四国地方 * [[丸亀城]][[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、128頁。"丸亀城跡東方の外堀跡" 　　[[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、138頁。"丸亀城南部の外堀跡と土塁" * [[高知城]] ;九州地方 * [[小倉城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"2004 平成16 小倉城（北九州市）外堀（総構）で障子堀検出 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[福岡城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、147頁。"福岡城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した外郭を持つ。中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[柳川城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、148頁。"柳川城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築された巨大な惣構を持つ。その中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[府内城]]（大分城）[[#木島2004|木島2004]]、151-152頁。"府内城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した巨大な構の中に、整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[中津城]][[#中村2017|中村2017]]、148頁。"豊前中津城（惣構え）（中略）『豊前中津之城圖』に描かれている要素から少なくとも図中の「惣構え」は寛永3年以前と推測できる。さらに古くならないものであろうか。" * [[山田城 (日向国)|山田城]][[#北郷1995|北郷1995]]、217頁。"「遠構」として外郭ラインを掘りきる遺構が存在し、山田町山田城では主郭を中心とした曲輪を大きく取り囲む形で一、三㎞に及ぶ堀が設定され、また[[都城市]]安永城郭跡では主郭から一㎞以上遠く離れた台地のくびれ部に堀を設ける例も見られる。" * [[安永城]] }} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[城郭]]における区画・区域|[[富山県]][[富山市]]の地名「総曲輪（そうがわ）」|総曲輪}} [[ファイル:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|江戸城総構え]] '''総構え'''（そうがまえ）は、[[城]]や[[砦]]の'''外郭'''（がいかく）、またはその囲まれた内部のこと。特に、城のほか[[城下町]]一帯も含めて外周を[[堀]]や[[石垣]]、[[土塁]]で囲い込んだ、日本の城郭構造をいう。'''惣構'''（そうがまえ）、'''総曲輪'''（そうぐるわ）、'''総郭'''（そうぐるわ）ともいう。 == 概要 == 日本では異民族の侵入が少なかったことや山地が国土の大半を占めることなどから、大陸に見られるような城壁都市は一部を除いて発展せず、野戦用の防御施設として作られた「[[城柵|柵]]」や武士の居館を堀や櫓で防備した「館（やかた、たて、たち）」が、中世には[[山城]]へと発展した。 近世にいたり、城郭が単なる軍事拠点のみならず政治的統治拠点としての役割を持つようになると、城下町や家臣団防備の目的で従来の城の機能的構成部分（内郭）から、さらにもう一重外側に防御線が設けられるようになった。これが総構えである。普通、「城」という場合は、内郭のみを指し、外郭である総構えは天然の地勢（山・河川）をも含むため、どこまでをいうのか不明瞭なものもあった。また総構えの堀は総堀（惣堀）と言うが、外堀と言われることも多い。ただし単に外堀と言った場合は、総構え堀を指す場合と本城の外側の堀を指す場合とがある。 [[後北条氏]]の拠点、[[小田原城]]の総構えは2里半（約9[[キロメートル|km]]）に及ぶ空堀と土塁で城下町全体を囲む長大なものであった{{refnest|group=注|小田原城の惣構について、[[西股総生]]は、 全体として集落の囲い込みよりも防御上の合理性に基づいて設定されたと評価している。[[板橋 (小田原市)|板橋]]の宿（中世東海道の宿）が惣構のラインからは除外される一方、城の東方は惣構を[[山王川]]のラインまでに設定しているが、山王川と城中心部との間にひろがる平野部に集落が密集しているとは考えにいことによる[[#西股2003|西股2003]]、27-29頁。}}。[[大坂城]]の外郭も周囲2里の長さで、[[大坂の陣#大坂冬の陣|大坂冬の陣]]では外郭南門の外側に出丸が造られ（[[真田丸]]）、徳川方は外郭内に1歩も侵入できなかったという。また江戸時代の江戸城外郭は最大で、堀・石垣・塀が渦状に配されて江戸市街の全てを囲んでいた。 総構えの典型は、中国の城や中世欧州の都市のように、都市全域を囲む堀と塁（城壁）にみることができる。中世都市の[[堺市|堺]]は三方を深さ3[[メートル|m]]、幅10m程の濠で囲み、木戸を設けて防御に備えていた。今でも遺構を見ることが出来る[[京都市|京都]]の「[[御土居]]」も典型的な総構えであり総延長は5里26町余（22.4km）にも及んだ{{refnest|group=注|「御土居」は、土塁とともに堀が構築されていた。土塁と堀の幅は各々約20mであった[[#中村2003|中村2003]]、316頁。}}。 ファイル:OdoiOmiya.jpg|[[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]大宮土居町の御土居堀 ファイル:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|姫路城総構えと街道沿いの町割り。文化3年（1806年）。 ファイル:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|[[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]]総構え
{{国土航空写真}} ファイル:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|福岡城の曲輪分界図 == 最古の遺構 == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部]] 現在判明している総構えの最古の遺構は、[[兵庫県]][[伊丹市]]にある[[伊丹城|有岡城]]（伊丹城）趾で、国指定の史跡1979年12月指定。『図説 中世城郭事典 三』p.36となっている『夢路を追って～官兵衛と村重～摂津⇔姫路』神戸新聞　2014.05.02 朝刊　20頁　西播。有岡城は、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]に伊丹氏により築城され伊丹城と称したが、[[天正]]年間に[[荒木村重]]により改修され総構えとなり有岡城と改称した。しかし10年ほどで廃城となっている。 尚、文明15年(1483年)に築城が始まった山科本願寺も巨大な土塁と水堀を用いて寺社町を囲う壮大な総構えを有しており{{refnest|group=注|山科本願寺は外郭線を三重にめぐらし、本願寺を核に求心的な構造をなしていた[[#福島2003|福島2003]]、327頁。}}、これを最古とする説もある。 File:Itamij4.jpg|文禄年間に伊丹の姿を描いた絵図 File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|現代の伊丹市の地図における総構えの範囲 {{-}} == 主な総構えの城 == {{Col| ;東北地方 * [[盛岡城]] * [[久保田城]] * [[米沢城]] * [[若松城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"1991 平成3 会津若松城総構に蒲生氏郷時代の大型障子堀 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[桧原城]][[#石田2009|石田2009]]、78頁。"[[伊達政宗]]は、桧原に桧原城築き、城下町を取り囲むように、直線的な外構を構築した。土塁と堀、[[虎口#枡形虎口|外桝形の虎口]]が築かれている。" ;関東地方 * [[水戸城]] * [[土浦城]] * [[館林城]] * [[倉賀野城]] * [[白井城]] | * [[宿阿内城]] * [[平井城]] * [[本佐倉城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"本佐倉城（[[千葉県]][[印旛郡]][[酒々井町]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[臼井城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"臼井城（千葉県[[佐倉市]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[岩槻城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、28頁。"岩槻城の惣構は、現在では市街地の中にごく一部を残すのみだが、戦前までは「岩槻の金屏風」と俗称される雄大な土塁が良好に遺存していた。" * [[滝の城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、19-20頁。"[[埼玉県]][[所沢市]]の滝ノ城も、長大な外郭線を有している。（中略）想定される敵主攻正面は北東方であり、そうした軍事的状況に対応するように外郭線が設定されている。この外郭線には数カ所に横矢の折れがあり、東面には横矢に援護された[[虎口#馬出|馬出]]も備えたらしい。（中略）技巧を駆使しているのは、広大な台地続きに対して防御線が長くならざるをえないことへの対処、と理解できる。" * [[江戸城]][[#岡田2008|岡田2008]]、35頁。"[[御茶ノ水]]の巨大な総構" * [[小田原城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、267頁。"1986 昭和61 小田原城北縁総構竜土院裏の発掘で障子堀確認 惣構障子堀の初見" ;中部地方 * [[甲府城]] * [[小岩嶽城]]{{refnest|group=注|小岩嶽城（現・[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]にある[[山城]]）には山麓部を囲繞する空堀（幅約15m・深さ約7m）がある[[#三島2000|三島2000]]、69-71頁。}} * [[長岡城]] * [[田中城]] | * [[西尾城]] * [[清洲城]] * [[犬山城]] * [[岐阜城]][[#髙田2003|髙田2003]]、66頁。"①外郭の堀・土塁は長良川沿岸部を除いてほぼ直線的なラインである。②外郭南側の端部は「権現山」裾部で終わっている。" * [[松ノ木城]][[#福島2000|福島2000]]、321頁。"松ノ木城の「外構」「惣構」" * [[金沢城]]＜天正一二年＞三月一三日付、羽柴秀吉書状『加越能古文叢』四一。"金沢之惣構" ;近畿地方 * [[桑名城]] * [[長浜城 (近江国)|長浜城]] * [[彦根城]] * [[伏見城]] * [[亀山城 (丹波国)|亀山城]] * [[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]] | * [[大坂城]][[#中井2008|中井2008]]、29頁。"豊臣秀吉の大坂城の構造（中略）本丸の外周に二の丸、三の丸が配され、さらにその外周は広大な総構となっていた。" * [[岸和田城]] * [[有岡城]] （伊丹城） * [[明石城]] * [[姫路城]][[#宮田2008|宮田2008]]、50-53頁。"西国将軍となった池田輝政の築いた姫路城は、長期の籠城も可能として町屋や寺町全域を外堀で囲い込んだ総構の城である。" ;中国地方 * [[岡山城]] * [[福山城 (備後国)|福山城]] * [[広島城]] * [[三次城]] * [[松江城]][[#徳永2016|徳永2016]]、259頁。"松江城下町遺跡の[[障子堀]]は、外堀のさらに外側に位置する" * [[萩城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、152頁。"萩城（中略）惣構：三ノ丸⑤を巨大な土塁・で固めた惣構とし、大身家臣団屋敷を包摂。外辺部曲輪⑥に町屋地区・中下級家臣団屋敷を包摂し、三ノ丸⑤と共に整備った（ママ）街区で集約化" | ;四国地方 * [[丸亀城]][[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、128頁。"丸亀城跡東方の外堀跡" 　　[[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、138頁。"丸亀城南部の外堀跡と土塁" * [[高知城]] ;九州地方 * [[小倉城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"2004 平成16 小倉城（北九州市）外堀（総構）で障子堀検出 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[福岡城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、147頁。"福岡城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した外郭を持つ。中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[柳川城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、148頁。"柳川城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築された巨大な惣構を持つ。その中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[府内城]]（大分城）[[#木島2004|木島2004]]、151-152頁。"府内城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した巨大な構の中に、整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[中津城]][[#中村2017|中村2017]]、148頁。"豊前中津城（惣構え）（中略）『豊前中津之城圖』に描かれている要素から少なくとも図中の「惣構え」は寛永3年以前と推測できる。さらに古くならないものであろうか。" * [[山田城 (日向国)|山田城]][[#北郷1995|北郷1995]]、217頁。"「遠構」として外郭ラインを掘りきる遺構が存在し、山田町山田城では主郭を中心とした曲輪を大きく取り囲む形で一、三㎞に及ぶ堀が設定され、また[[都城市]]安永城郭跡では主郭から一㎞以上遠く離れた台地のくびれ部に堀を設ける例も見られる。" * [[安永城]] }} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{otheruses|[[城郭]]における区画・区域|[[富山県]][[富山市]]の地名「総曲輪（そうがわ）」|総曲輪}} [[ファイル:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|江戸城総構え]] '''総構え'''（そうがまえ）は、[[城]]や[[砦]]の'''外郭'''（がいかく）、またはその囲まれた内部のこと。特に、城のほか[[城下町]]一帯も含めて外周を[[堀]]や[[石垣]]、[[土塁]]で囲い込んだ、日本の城郭構造をいう。'''惣構'''（そうがまえ）、'''総曲輪'''（そうぐるわ）、'''総郭'''（そうぐるわ）ともいう。 == 概要 == 日本では異民族の侵入が少なかったことや山地が国土の大半を占めることなどから、大陸に見られるような城壁都市は一部を除いて発展せず、野戦用の防御施設として作られた「[[城柵|柵]]」や武士の居館を堀や櫓で防備した「館（やかた、たて、たち）」が、中世には[[山城]]へと発展した。 近世にいたり、城郭が単なる軍事拠点のみならず政治的統治拠点としての役割を持つようになると、城下町や家臣団防備の目的で従来の城の機能的構成部分（内郭）から、さらにもう一重外側に防御線が設けられるようになった。これが総構えである。普通、「城」という場合は、内郭のみを指し、外郭である総構えは天然の地勢（山・河川）をも含むため、どこまでをいうのか不明瞭なものもあった。また総構えの堀は総堀（惣堀）と言うが、外堀と言われることも多い。ただし単に外堀と言った場合は、総構え堀を指す場合と本城の外側の堀を指す場合とがある。 [[後北条氏]]の拠点、[[小田原城]]の総構えは2里半（約9[[キロメートル|km]]）に及ぶ空堀と土塁で城下町全体を囲む長大なものであった{{refnest|group=注|小田原城の惣構について、[[西股総生]]は、 全体として集落の囲い込みよりも防御上の合理性に基づいて設定されたと評価している。[[板橋 (小田原市)|板橋]]の宿（中世東海道の宿）が惣構のラインからは除外される一方、城の東方は惣構を[[山王川]]のラインまでに設定しているが、山王川と城中心部との間にひろがる平野部に集落が密集しているとは考えにいことによる[[#西股2003|西股2003]]、27-29頁。}}。[[大坂城]]の外郭も周囲2里の長さで、[[大坂の陣#大坂冬の陣|大坂冬の陣]]では外郭南門の外側に出丸が造られ（[[真田丸]]）、徳川方は外郭内に1歩も侵入できなかったという。また江戸時代の江戸城外郭は最大で、堀・石垣・塀が渦状に配されて江戸市街の全てを囲んでいた。 総構えの典型は、中国の城や中世欧州の都市のように、都市全域を囲む堀と塁（城壁）にみることができる。中世都市の[[堺市|堺]]は三方を深さ3[[メートル|m]]、幅10m程の濠で囲み、木戸を設けて防御に備えていた。今でも遺構を見ることが出来る[[京都市|京都]]の「[[御土居]]」も典型的な総構えであり総延長は5里26町余（22.4km）にも及んだ{{refnest|group=注|「御土居」は、土塁とともに堀が構築されていた。土塁と堀の幅は各々約20mであった[[#中村2003|中村2003]]、316頁。}}。 ファイル:OdoiOmiya.jpg|[[京都市]][[北区 (京都市)|北区]]大宮土居町の御土居堀 ファイル:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|姫路城総構えと街道沿いの町割り。文化3年（1806年）。 ファイル:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|[[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]]総構え
{{国土航空写真}} ファイル:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|福岡城の曲輪分界図 == 最古の遺構 == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部]] 現在判明している総構えの最古の遺構は、[[兵庫県]][[伊丹市]]にある[[伊丹城|有岡城]]（伊丹城）趾で、国指定の史跡1979年12月指定。『図説 中世城郭事典 三』p.36となっている『夢路を追って～官兵衛と村重～摂津⇔姫路』神戸新聞　2014.05.02 朝刊　20頁　西播。有岡城は、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]に伊丹氏により築城され伊丹城と称したが、[[天正]]年間に[[荒木村重]]により改修され総構えとなり有岡城と改称した。しかし10年ほどで廃城となっている。 尚、文明15年(1483年)に築城が始まった山科本願寺も巨大な土塁と水堀を用いて寺社町を囲う壮大な総構えを有しており{{refnest|group=注|山科本願寺は外郭線を三重にめぐらし、本願寺を核に求心的な構造をなしていた[[#福島2003|福島2003]]、327頁。}}、これを最古とする説もある。 File:Itamij4.jpg|文禄年間に伊丹の姿を描いた絵図 File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|現代の伊丹市の地図における総構えの範囲 {{-}} == 主な総構えの城 == {{Col| ;東北地方 * [[盛岡城]] * [[久保田城]] * [[米沢城]] * [[若松城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"1991 平成3 会津若松城総構に蒲生氏郷時代の大型障子堀 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[桧原城]][[#石田2009|石田2009]]、78頁。"[[伊達政宗]]は、桧原に桧原城築き、城下町を取り囲むように、直線的な外構を構築した。土塁と堀、[[虎口#枡形虎口|外桝形の虎口]]が築かれている。" ;関東地方 * [[水戸城]] * [[土浦城]] * [[館林城]] * [[倉賀野城]] * [[白井城]] | * [[宿阿内城]] * [[平井城]] * [[本佐倉城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"本佐倉城（[[千葉県]][[印旛郡]][[酒々井町]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[臼井城]][[#柴田2003|柴田2003]]、321頁。"臼井城（千葉県[[佐倉市]]）（中略）惣構ライン上に砦群を配置" * [[岩槻城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、28頁。"岩槻城の惣構は、現在では市街地の中にごく一部を残すのみだが、戦前までは「岩槻の金屏風」と俗称される雄大な土塁が良好に遺存していた。" * [[滝の城]][[#西股2003|西股2003]]、19-20頁。"[[埼玉県]][[所沢市]]の滝ノ城も、長大な外郭線を有している。（中略）想定される敵主攻正面は北東方であり、そうした軍事的状況に対応するように外郭線が設定されている。この外郭線には数カ所に横矢の折れがあり、東面には横矢に援護された[[虎口#馬出|馬出]]も備えたらしい。（中略）技巧を駆使しているのは、広大な台地続きに対して防御線が長くならざるをえないことへの対処、と理解できる。" * [[江戸城]][[#岡田2008|岡田2008]]、35頁。"[[御茶ノ水]]の巨大な総構" * [[小田原城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、267頁。"1986 昭和61 小田原城北縁総構竜土院裏の発掘で障子堀確認 惣構障子堀の初見" ;中部地方 * [[甲府城]] * [[小岩嶽城]]{{refnest|group=注|小岩嶽城（現・[[長野県]][[安曇野市]]にある[[山城]]）には山麓部を囲繞する空堀（幅約15m・深さ約7m）がある[[#三島2000|三島2000]]、69-71頁。}} * [[長岡城]] * [[田中城]] | * [[西尾城]] * [[清洲城]] * [[犬山城]] * [[岐阜城]][[#髙田2003|髙田2003]]、66頁。"①外郭の堀・土塁は長良川沿岸部を除いてほぼ直線的なラインである。②外郭南側の端部は「権現山」裾部で終わっている。" * [[松ノ木城]][[#福島2000|福島2000]]、321頁。"松ノ木城の「外構」「惣構」" * [[金沢城]]＜天正一二年＞三月一三日付、羽柴秀吉書状『加越能古文叢』四一。"金沢之惣構" ;近畿地方 * [[桑名城]] * [[長浜城 (近江国)|長浜城]] * [[彦根城]] * [[伏見城]] * [[亀山城 (丹波国)|亀山城]] * [[郡山城 (大和国)|郡山城]] | * [[大坂城]][[#中井2008|中井2008]]、29頁。"豊臣秀吉の大坂城の構造（中略）本丸の外周に二の丸、三の丸が配され、さらにその外周は広大な総構となっていた。" * [[岸和田城]] * [[有岡城]] （伊丹城） * [[明石城]] * [[姫路城]][[#宮田2008|宮田2008]]、50-53頁。"西国将軍となった池田輝政の築いた姫路城は、長期の籠城も可能として町屋や寺町全域を外堀で囲い込んだ総構の城である。" ;中国地方 * [[岡山城]] * [[福山城 (備後国)|福山城]] * [[広島城]] * [[三次城]] * [[松江城]][[#徳永2016|徳永2016]]、259頁。"松江城下町遺跡の[[障子堀]]は、外堀のさらに外側に位置する" * [[萩城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、152頁。"萩城（中略）惣構：三ノ丸⑤を巨大な土塁・で固めた惣構とし、大身家臣団屋敷を包摂。外辺部曲輪⑥に町屋地区・中下級家臣団屋敷を包摂し、三ノ丸⑤と共に整備った（ママ）街区で集約化" | ;四国地方 * [[丸亀城]][[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、128頁。"丸亀城跡東方の外堀跡" 　　[[#髙田2019|髙田2019]]、138頁。"丸亀城南部の外堀跡と土塁" * [[高知城]] ;九州地方 * [[小倉城]][[#小笠原2016|小笠原2016]]、268頁。"2004 平成16 小倉城（北九州市）外堀（総構）で障子堀検出 天正18年[[小田原合戦]]後" * [[福岡城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、147頁。"福岡城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した外郭を持つ。中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[柳川城]][[#木島2004|木島2004]]、148頁。"柳川城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築された巨大な惣構を持つ。その中には整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[府内城]]（大分城）[[#木島2004|木島2004]]、151-152頁。"府内城（中略）惣構：堀・土塁で構築した巨大な構の中に、整然とした街区で集約化した士分屋敷・町屋地区を包摂" * [[中津城]][[#中村2017|中村2017]]、148頁。"豊前中津城（惣構え）（中略）『豊前中津之城圖』に描かれている要素から少なくとも図中の「惣構え」は寛永3年以前と推測できる。さらに古くならないものであろうか。" * [[山田城 (日向国)|山田城]][[#北郷1995|北郷1995]]、217頁。"「遠構」として外郭ラインを掘りきる遺構が存在し、山田町山田城では主郭を中心とした曲輪を大きく取り囲む形で一、三㎞に及ぶ堀が設定され、また[[都城市]]安永城郭跡では主郭から一㎞以上遠く離れた台地のくびれ部に堀を設ける例も見られる。" * [[安永城]] }} == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === Digital Gallery) |date=20071014181902}} * {{Cite conference |df=ja|conferenceurl=https://www.komazawa-u.ac.jp/~kazov/chujoken/semi2002/ |conference=第19回全国城郭研究者セミナー |title=「惣構」の再検討 revision.text:
[[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|qq=|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|qq=|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * [[:en:Kuruwa|Kuruwa]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Moated settlement]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Monk-soldier]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Enclosed settlements]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * [[:en:Kuruwa|Kuruwa]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Moated settlement]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Monk-soldier]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Enclosed settlements]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺社勢力|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine power|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}/{{ill|10=ja|11=アジール|Asylum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=asylum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26377}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Warrior monks]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} [[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo (Kyuhoji Jinaicho in Yao City, Osaka Prefecture)]] * {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Jōkamachi]] [[Category:Castles in Japan]] }} [[ja:総構え]] {{translated page|ja|総構え|version=105451432|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11607658}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * [[:en:Kuruwa|Kuruwa]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Moated settlement]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Monk-soldier]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Enclosed settlements]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple {{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
[[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * [[:en:Kuruwa|Kuruwa]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Moated settlement]] * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Monk-soldier]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Enclosed settlements]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple power|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}/{{ill|10=ja|11=アジール|Asylum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=asylum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26377}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Warrior monks]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} [[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo
