revision.text:
[[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a [[:en:Castle|Castle]] or [[:en:Fortress|Fortress]], or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the [[:en:Castle town|Castle town]] and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * [[:en:Kuruwa|Kuruwa]] {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Moated settlement]] * [[:en:Holy city|Holy city]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Monk-soldier]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Moated settlements|Enclosed settlements]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive structures|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺社勢力|Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and Shrine Powers|lt=Temple and shrine power|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}/{{ill|10=ja|11=アジール|Asylum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|1=asylum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q26377}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * [[:en:Sōhei|Warrior monks]] * {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} [[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]], a stronghold of the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]], was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of [[:en:Sakai|Sakai]] was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The [[:en:Odoi|Odoi]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in [[:en:Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita-ku, Kyoto]] File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of [[:en:Fukuoka Castle|Fukuoka Castle]] == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by [[:en:Araki Murashige|Araki Murashige]] during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]][[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * [[:en:Odawara Castle|Odawara Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * and Kinki|lt=Kinai and nearby regions during the Warring States period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} [[File:Isshinden Jinaicho.JPG|thumb|300px|Jinaicho formed around the main temple of Jōdo [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a {{ill|Castle|en|Castle|WD=Q23413|lt=Castle}} or {{ill|Fortress|en|Fortress|WD=Q57831|lt=Fortress}}, or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the {{ill|Castle town|en|Castle town|WD=Q21086453|lt=Castle town}} and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}, a stronghold of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}}, was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The {{ill|Odoi|en|Odoi|WD=Q37530302|lt=Odoi}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... The {{ill|Odoi|en|Odoi|WD=Q37530302|lt=Odoi}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated revision.text:
[[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a {{ill|Castle|en|Castle|WD=Q23413|lt=Castle}} or {{ill|Fortress|en|Fortress|WD=Q57831|lt=Fortress}}, or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the {{ill|Castle town|en|Castle town|WD=Q21086453|lt=Castle town}} and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}, a stronghold of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}}, was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The {{ill|Odoi|en|Odoi|WD=Q37530302|lt=Odoi}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=Kuruwa}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|WD=Q60995739|lt=Moated settlement}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=Sogo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総構え}} * {{ill|Holy city|en|Holy city|WD=Q692581|lt=Holy city}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=門前町|Monzen-machi|lt=Monzen-machi|lt=Temple towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=寺町|Teramachi|lt=Teramachi|lt=Temple {{ill|10=ja|11=畿内・近国の戦国時代|The Warring States Period in Kinai and Kinki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|Moated settlements|en|Moated settlements|WD=Q60995739|lt=Enclosed settlements}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=城郭都市|Walled City|lt=Walled City|lt=Castle towns|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=惣構|Sōgamae|lt=Sōgamae|lt=General defensive However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated revision.text:
[[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a {{ill|Castle|en|Castle|WD=Q23413|lt=Castle}} or {{ill|Fortress|en|Fortress|WD=Q57831|lt=Fortress}}, or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the {{ill|Castle town|en|Castle town|WD=Q21086453|lt=Castle town}} and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}, a stronghold of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan}}, was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai}} was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The {{ill|Odoi|en|Odoi|WD=Q37530302|lt=Odoi}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=Moated Towns|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle|1=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] [[ja:総構え]] {{otheruses|[[:en:Castle walls|Castle walls]] and their enclosures|The place name "Sōgawa" in [[:en:Toyama City|Toyama City]], {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture|1=Toyama Prefecture}}|総曲輪}} {{Special characters|description=[[Microsoft Code Page 932]] (Hagoromo High)}} [[File:Edo hori.png|thumb|250px|right|The "Sōgamae" of Edo Castle]] '''Sōgamae''' (総構え) refers to the '''outer enclosure''' of a {{ill|Castle|en|Castle|WD=Q23413|lt=Castle|1=Castle}} or {{ill|Fortress|en|Fortress|WD=Q57831|lt=Fortress|1=Fortress}}, or the area enclosed within it. In Japan, it particularly denotes a castle structure where the outer perimeter, including the {{ill|Castle town|en|Castle town|WD=Q21086453|lt=Castle town|1=Castle town}} and the retainers' quarters, is surrounded by [[:en:Moats|Moats]], [[:en:Stone walls|Stone walls]], and [[earthen embankments]]. It is also called '''Sōkō''' (惣構), '''Sōguruwa''' (総曲輪), or '''Sōguruwa''' (総郭). == Overview == In Japan, factors such as limited incursions by foreign forces and the country's mountainous terrain meant that walled cities, common on the continent, did not develop extensively, with some exceptions. Instead, fortifications such as [[:en:palisades|palisades]] and "yakata" (fortified residences of warriors protected by moats and towers) evolved during the [[:en:Medieval Japan|medieval period]] into [[mountain castles]]. By the early modern period, castles became not only military outposts but also political and administrative centers. To defend the castle town and the residences of retainers, an additional outer line of defense was built beyond the inner defensive structures (inner enclosure). This outer defense is what is referred to as "Sōgamae." Typically, "castle" refers only to the inner enclosure, while the "Sōgamae" often included natural terrain such as mountains and rivers, making its boundaries ambiguous. The moat of a "Sōgamae" is called a "Sōbori" (惣堀) but is often referred to as the "outer moat." However, the term "outer moat" can sometimes refer to the moat outside the castle's main enclosure rather than the "Sōgamae" moat. The "Sōgamae" of {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle|1=Odawara Castle}}, a stronghold of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}}, was surrounded by trenches and embankments that stretched for 2.5 leagues (approximately 9 km), enclosing the entire castle town. {{refnest|group=Note|According to [[Tsunao Nishimata]], the layout of Odawara Castle’s Sōgamae was not primarily for enclosing the settlement but was rationally designed for defense. For example, the [[Itabashi]] post station (a medieval [[Tokaido]] inn) was excluded from the Sōgamae line, while the eastern boundary extended to the [[Sannogawa]] river, despite the plains between the river and the castle center not being densely populated.[[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], pp. 27–29.}} The outer enclosure of {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}} also extended for 2 leagues. During the [[Siege of Osaka#Winter Campaign|Winter Campaign of the Siege of Osaka]], an outwork called [[:en:Sanadamaru|Sanadamaru]] was constructed outside the southern gate of the outer enclosure, preventing the [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa forces]] from breaching the enclosure. The outer enclosure of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} was the largest, with its moats, stone walls, and fences forming a spiral pattern that encompassed the entire city of Edo. A typical example of a "Sōgamae" can be found in the [[Chinese castles]] and medieval European cities, which were surrounded by moats and ramparts (castle walls). The medieval city of {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|WD=Q193428|lt=Sakai|1=Sakai}} was enclosed on three sides by moats 3 meters deep and 10 meters wide, with gates for defense. The {{ill|Odoi|en|Odoi|WD=Q37530302|lt=Odoi|1=Odoi}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, a typical "Sōgamae," had a total length of 5 leagues and 26 town blocks (22.4 km). {{refnest|group=Note|The "Odoi" included embankments and moats, each approximately 20 meters wide.[[#Nakamura2003|Nakamura 2003]], p. 316.}} File:OdoiOmiya.jpg|The Odoi moat in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto|1=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} File:Himeji-castletown-plan.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[Himeji Castle]] and the town layout along the main roads (Culture Year 3, 1806) File:Kooriyamajyou soukuruwa.jpg|The "Sōgamae" of [[:en:Koriyama Castle|Koriyama Castle]]
{{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle|1=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige|1=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle|1=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ... (continuing for the other regions) == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group=Note}} === Citations === {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Moated Towns|ja|環濠都市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moated Towns|1=moated town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11574117}} * {{ill|Odoi|ja|御土居|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Odoi|1=Odoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488867}} * {{ill|Kuruwa|en|Kuruwa|WD=Q6447542|lt=Kuruwa|1=Kuruwa}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaimachi|lt=Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaicho|qq=}} * {{ill|Moated districts|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=境内都市|Temple Precinct Cities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|Sōhei|en|Sōhei|WD=Q1475695|lt=Monk-soldier|1=Sōhei}} * {{National Aerial Photography}} File:福岡城曲輪分界図.png|A diagram of the sections of {{ill|Fukuoka Castle|en|Fukuoka Castle|WD=Q1143944|lt=Fukuoka Castle|1=Fukuoka Castle}} == Oldest Remains == [[File:有岡城の総構石垣遺構の一部.jpg|thumb|Part of the "Sōgamae" stone wall remains of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]]]] The oldest known "Sōgamae" remains are those of [[:en:Arioka Castle|Arioka Castle]] (Itami Castle) in [[:en:Itami, Hyōgo|Itami, Hyōgo]], designated as a National Historic Site in 1979.‘Illustrated Medieval Castle Dictionary, Volume 3,’ p.36 Originally built by the [[Itami clan]] during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, it was later renovated into a "Sōgamae" by {{ill|Araki Murashige|en|Araki Murashige|WD=Q2859472|lt=Araki Murashige|1=Araki Murashige}} during the [[Tenshō era]], and renamed "Arioka Castle." However, the castle was abandoned after just a decade. Additionally, the [[Yamashina Honganji]], built starting in 1483, featured a grand "Sōgamae" that surrounded its temple town with massive embankments and moats. {{refnest|group=Note|The Yamashina Honganji had three concentric outer enclosures, forming a centripetal structure around the Honganji temple.[[#Fukushima2003|Fukushima 2003]], p. 327.}} File:Itamij4.jpg|A drawing of Itami during the Bunroku era File:伊丹郷町の範囲.png|Modern map of Itami City showing the extent of the "Sōgamae" {{-}} == Major Examples of "Sōgamae" Castles == {{Col| ; Tohoku Region * [[Morioka Castle]] * [[Kubota Castle]] * [[Yonezawa Castle]] * [[Aizu-Wakamatsu Castle]][[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 268. * [[Hibara Castle]][[#Ishida2009|Ishida 2009]], p. 78. ; Kanto Region * [[Mito Castle]] * [[Tsuchiura Castle]] * [[Tatebayashi Castle]] * [[Kuragano Castle]] * [[Shiroi Castle]] | * [[Shukauchi Castle]] * [[Hirai Castle]] * [[Hon-Sakura Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Usui Castle]][[#Shibata2003|Shibata 2003]], p. 321. * [[Iwatsuki Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 28. * [[Takino Castle]][[#Nishimata2003|Nishimata 2003]], p. 19-20. * {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}}[[#Okada2008|Okada 2008]], p. 35. * {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|WD=Q1013437|lt=Odawara Castle|1=Odawara Castle}}[[#Ogasawara2016|Ogasawara 2016]], p. 267. }} ...
